summary: I have spent much of my career in Alabama, working to modernize and reform the state's judicial system. Around the early 1970s, we achieved a major constitutional revision with a new judicial article passed in 1973, the first significant change since 1901. Alabama's judicial politics remain largely elective, but we made strides in court management and procedure reform. The state's largest counties, including Jefferson County and Birmingham, were central to these efforts.

I served as president of the Alabama State Bar from 1965 to 1966, which was a pivotal time for me. During that period, I initiated what I considered a reform movement within the courts and the bar profession in Alabama. This role helped shape my interest in judicial modernization and reform, which later influenced my run for chief justice. The bar association was also a key supporter in the judicial reform campaigns.

Governor Patterson was my opponent when I ran for chief justice in Alabama. He had announced his candidacy before I decided to run, though I had encouraged others to enter the race as well. Despite his status as a former governor, I was persuaded by many lawyers statewide to run, and I saw it as an opportunity to implement long-held judicial reforms. The campaign was challenging but ultimately successful against him.

As chief justice, one of my priorities was to clear the dockets of the appellate courts, including the Alabama Court of Criminal Appeals. By the new term in 1973, we had brought all appellate courts current, which was a significant achievement. This court, alongside the Court of Civil Appeals, benefited from the new judicial article and reforms that improved case management and procedural rules in Alabama.

The Alabama Court of Civil Appeals was part of the judicial system reforms I worked on as chief justice. We focused on clearing its docket and implementing new procedural rules modeled after the federal rules of civil procedure. These efforts were part of a broader modernization that culminated in the 1973 judicial article, which revamped Alabama's court system substantially.

The Alabama Constitutional Commission was created in 1969 and worked for several years to draft a new constitution for the state. They completed their work around 1973, and their judicial article was the first part to be adopted by voters. I collaborated closely with the commission and other groups to push this reform through, which was a major step toward modernizing Alabama's government structure.

Jefferson County, home to Birmingham, is the largest county in Alabama and played a key role in judicial selection reforms. It had a nominating commission system where a five-person commission selected three candidates for the governor to appoint from. This system influenced the judicial article amendments and served as a model for other counties seeking local legislation for judicial appointments.

Birmingham, the largest city in Alabama and located in Jefferson County, was central to judicial reform efforts. The city’s judicial selection system influenced statewide changes, especially regarding the appointment and election of judges. My campaign and judicial work often intersected with Birmingham’s legal community and political environment.

The Missouri Plan was proposed by the Alabama Constitutional Commission as a model for appointing judges, though not for their retention. It involves a nominating commission selecting candidates for the governor to appoint. However, the Alabama legislature rejected full adoption of this plan, preferring to maintain an elected judiciary system, especially due to opposition from the governor and his lieutenants.

The League of Women Voters was one of the many organizations that supported the passage of Alabama's new judicial article in the early 1970s. Their involvement was notable because it was the first time in Alabama history that such diverse groups—including the League, Farm Bureau, and major black leaders—came together on a single issue. Their advocacy helped build broad-based support for judicial reform.

The Farm Bureau was part of the coalition supporting Alabama's judicial reform in the early 1970s. Alongside groups like the League of Women Voters and major black leaders, they helped create a broad base of support for the new judicial article. This unusual alliance of diverse interests was crucial in overcoming opposition from some judges and lawyers.

Jim Folsom, a former governor of Alabama, campaigned heavily against the new judicial article despite his historically progressive ideas. I believe he didn't fully understand the reform, which was unfortunate because his support could have been influential. His opposition was part of the broader resistance from some lawyers and judges to the changes we were trying to implement.

After my time in Alabama, I ended up running for chief judge of the New York Court of Appeals, which is the highest court in New York. The campaign there was intense and expensive, with unofficial reports saying each candidate's supporters spent over a million dollars. This contrasted sharply with my Alabama campaign, highlighting the challenges of judicial elections in larger states.

I moved to New York to run for chief judge of the New York Court of Appeals. The judicial elections there were very costly and politically charged, with candidates' supporters spending over a million dollars each. This experience showed me the difficulties and ethical challenges of judicial campaigns in a large, populous state compared to Alabama.

I visited New Orleans shortly after an election to speak at a judges' meeting. There, I learned that judicial candidates in New Orleans districts had supporters who spent around $200,000 each on campaigns. This was another example of how judicial elections in some places have become very expensive and politically intense, which concerns me for the integrity of the judiciary.

During my visit to New Orleans, I encountered the judicial election environment under Mayor Landry's administration. The high campaign spending and political involvement in judicial races there were notable. Although I did not discuss Mayor Landry extensively, his tenure contextualized the political climate of the city at that time.

Leon Sarpy was a former law partner of one of the judicial candidates in New Orleans whom I met during my visit. His connection to the candidate highlighted the close ties within the legal community there. This underscored the personal and political networks involved in judicial campaigns in that region.

Garrison was mentioned in the context of judicial elections and campaigns, but I did not have a direct relationship or detailed involvement with him. His name came up as part of the broader discussion about judicial politics and campaign spending in various states. I recognize the challenges these figures face in balancing politics and judicial duties.

Marcus was referenced in the discussion of judicial elections, particularly in New Orleans, but I did not have a personal connection with him. His role was part of the broader judicial and political environment I observed during my visits and campaigns. These experiences informed my views on the complexities of judicial selection processes.

Mobile, Alabama, was part of the local areas where campaign contributions came from during my run for chief justice. Citizens and friends there raised money out of local pride for a native son running for office. This grassroots support was important in balancing the larger statewide campaign efforts.
